blocks.statement: I can compute a_c = v²/r and F_c = m·v²/r for an object in circular motion and explain why F_c always points toward the center.

blocks.whatWeKnow: An orbit IS circular motion. The force that holds an orbit is gravity.
blocks.connection: Today we get the centripetal math. Tomorrow we connect it to gravity and discover that orbits ARE FALLING.

blocks.markdown:
## Turning is accelerating

An object moving in a circle at constant **speed** is still accelerating — its **direction** is changing every instant. That acceleration points toward the center:

- **a_c = v² / r** — centripetal acceleration
- **F_c = m·v² / r** — the net inward force required to keep the circle

Faster (bigger v) or tighter (smaller r) → more force needed. If the available force can't supply F_c, the object leaves the circle.

terms.definition: The NET inward force that keeps an object on a circular path; F_c = m·v²/r. A car turning a corner: friction is the centripetal force. A satellite orbiting: gravity is.
terms.cognate: Sp. fuerza centrípeta · Pt. força centrípeta · HC fòs santipèt

blocks.title: Centripetal force is a ROLE, not a new force
blocks.markdown: It's not a new kind of force. It's the role played by an **existing** force — friction, gravity, tension — whenever that force points toward the center. And when the string cuts, the ball flies along the **TANGENT** — not outward. There's no 'centrifugal force' pushing it out; the inward force just disappears.

blocks.title: Ball on a string — the two arrows that matter
blocks.caption: v points along the tangent (where the ball would go if released). F_c points inward along the string. If the string cuts, the ball follows v — straight out along the tangent.
blocks.genPrompt: A ball in circular motion: velocity vector tangent to the circle, centripetal force vector pointing toward the center, 90 degrees apart.

blocks.prompt: A 1500 kg car rounds a 50 m radius curve at 20 m/s. What centripetal force must friction provide?
blocks.answer: 12,000 N inward. Friction has to provide all of it — if it can't, the car skids out.

blocks.prompt: A 0.2 kg ball moves in a horizontal circle at 4 m/s on a 1 m string. What is the centripetal force? What provides it?
blocks.givenHint: m = 0.2 kg, v = 4 m/s, r = 1 m. Name the force playing the centripetal role in your Answer.

blocks.prompt: The string cuts while the ball is circling. Which way does the ball fly, and why is 'it gets flung outward by centrifugal force' the wrong explanation?
blocks.frame: The ball flies along the ___, because the only thing that changed is that the ___ force disappeared.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **10.1–10.5** (rotation vs. revolution, rotational and tangential speed, centripetal force, and why 'centrifugal force' isn't a true force). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
